questions.text: An attacker modifies a transaction in Block #500 of a 1,000-block chain. What is the MINIMUM additional work required to make their tampered chain "valid"?

options.text: Recompute the hashes of Block #500 through Block #1,000 - all 501 blocks
Recompute only the blocks before Block #500
No additional work - just change the data and the network accepts it

questions.explanation: Changing any data in Block #500 changes Block #500's hash. Block #501's PrevHash now points to a hash that no longer exists - so Block #501 is invalid. To fix Block #501, the attacker must recompute its hash too (with the correct new PrevHash) - but now Block #502's PrevHash doesn't match Block #501's new hash. This cascade continues all the way to Block #1,000. Every block from the tampered one to the tip of the chain must be recomputed. AND this must be done faster than the honest network continues adding new honest blocks.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - Explanation

Changing any data in Block #500 changes Block #500's hash. Block #501's PrevHash now points to a hash that no longer exists - so Block #501 is invalid. To fix Block #501, the attacker must recompute its hash too (with the correct new PrevHash) - but now Block #502's PrevHash doesn't match Block #501's new hash. This cascade continues all the way to Block #1,000. Every block from the tampered one to the tip of the chain must be recomputed. AND this must be done faster than the honest network continues adding new honest blocks.

questions.text: Which of the following is a correct description of a "51% attack"?

options.text: Stealing the private keys of 51% of all wallet holders on the network
Controlling more than half the network's consensus power to build an alternative chain faster than the honest network, enabling double-spending of recent transactions
Creating 51% more coins than the block reward allows
Causing 51% of all transactions to fail by sending invalid data to nodes

questions.explanation: A 51% attack requires controlling >50% of the network's consensus power (hashrate in PoW, stake in PoS). With this majority, the attacker can build an alternative chain faster than the honest majority - and since blockchains follow the "longest valid chain" rule, the attacker's chain is accepted. This enables double-spending of recent transactions. It does NOT allow: stealing funds from other wallets (requires those wallets' private keys), creating coins beyond protocol rules, or reaching far back into history.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - Explanation

A 51% attack requires controlling >50% of the network's consensus power (hashrate in PoW, stake in PoS). With this majority, the attacker can build an alternative chain faster than the honest majority - and since blockchains follow the "longest valid chain" rule, the attacker's chain is accepted. This enables double-spending of recent transactions. It does NOT allow: stealing funds from other wallets (requires those wallets' private keys), creating coins beyond protocol rules, or reaching far back into history.

questions.text: "Blockchain immutability is enforced by legal frameworks and access control policies - similar to how a traditional database is made tamper-proof. Blockchain is simply a more legally protected database."
questions.explanation:
1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.
        1 mark: Explains that traditional database immutability is policy-based (administrators can bypass it), while blockchain immutability is structural and cryptographic.
        1 mark: Explains the specific mechanism - hash chaining creates a cascade of invalidation that requires recomputing all subsequent blocks, making alteration computationally infeasible at scale.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.

1 mark: Explains that traditional database immutability is policy-based (administrators can bypass it), while blockchain immutability is structural and cryptographic.

1 mark: Explains the specific mechanism - hash chaining creates a cascade of invalidation that requires recomputing all subsequent blocks, making alteration computationally infeasible at scale.

Zero marks for stating FALSE without justification, or for justification that only mentions "cryptography is strong" without explaining the hash chain mechanism.

questions.text:
A blockchain inspector presents you with the following 5-block chain. Analyse the data carefully and answer the questions below.

Block #1: Data = "TX: A→B ₹100" | Own Hash = "aaa111" | PrevHash = "000000"
Block #2: Data = "TX: C→D ₹200" | Own Hash = "bbb222" | PrevHash = "aaa111"
Block #3: Data = "TX: E→F ₹300" | Own Hash = "xyz999" | PrevHash = "bbb222"
Block #4: Data = "TX: G→H ₹400" | Own Hash = "ddd444" | PrevHash = "xyz999"
Block #5: Data = "TX: I→J ₹500" | Own Hash = "eee555" | PrevHash = "ccc333"

Note: In a valid chain, each block's PrevHash must equal the previous block's Own Hash.

(a) Identify which block was tampered with. (1 mark)
(b) Explain exactly what evidence reveals the tampering. (2 marks)
(c) What should Block #5's PrevHash correctly be, for the B4→B5 link to be valid? (1 mark)
(d) How many blocks need to be recomputed by an attacker trying to "fix" this tampered chain from Block #3 onwards? (1 mark)
questions.explanation:
(a) Block #3 was tampered with. [1 mark]
(b) Evidence of tampering: [2 marks]
First, check the B2→B3 link: Block #3's PrevHash = "bbb222" ✅ - this matches Block #2's Own Hash. So far so good.
Next, check B3→B4: Block #4's PrevHash = "xyz999" ✅ - this matches Block #3's Own Hash. Also fine.
Now check B4→B5: Block #5's PrevHash = "ccc333" ❌ - Block #4's Own Hash is "ddd444", NOT "ccc333". This is the mismatch.
Tracing back: Block #5's PrevHash says it expects Block #4 to have hash "ccc333". But Block #4's actual Own Hash is "ddd444". This means Block #4's data or PrevHash was altered after Block #5 was created - changing its Own Hash from what was originally "ccc333" to the current "ddd444". The original pre-tamper hash of Block #3's data would have produced an Own Hash of "ccc333" - meaning Block #3's data was altered, producing a new Own Hash of "xyz999" (instead of the original value that would have led to Block #4's hash being "ccc333" and Block #5's PrevHash matching).
(c) Block #5's PrevHash should be "ddd444" [1 mark] - Block #4's actual Own Hash - for the B4→B5 link to be valid.
(d) Blocks to recompute: [1 mark] Starting from Block #3 (tampered), the attacker must recompute: Block #3's hash, then Block #4's hash (which depends on Block #3's new PrevHash), then Block #5's hash. That is 3 blocks (Blocks #3, #4, and #5).
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Model Answer - 5 Marks

(a) Block #3 was tampered with. [1 mark]


(b) Evidence of tampering: [2 marks]

First, check the B2→B3 link: Block #3's PrevHash = "bbb222" ✅ - this matches Block #2's Own Hash. So far so good.

Next, check B3→B4: Block #4's PrevHash = "xyz999" ✅ - this matches Block #3's Own Hash. Also fine.

Now check B4→B5: Block #5's PrevHash = "ccc333" ❌ - Block #4's Own Hash is "ddd444", NOT "ccc333". This is the mismatch.

Tracing back: Block #5's PrevHash says it expects Block #4 to have hash "ccc333". But Block #4's actual Own Hash is "ddd444". This means Block #4's data or PrevHash was altered after Block #5 was created - changing its Own Hash from what was originally "ccc333" to the current "ddd444". The original pre-tamper hash of Block #3's data would have produced an Own Hash of "ccc333" - meaning Block #3's data was altered, producing a new Own Hash of "xyz999" (instead of the original value that would have led to Block #4's hash being "ccc333" and Block #5's PrevHash matching).


(c) Block #5's PrevHash should be "ddd444" [1 mark] - Block #4's actual Own Hash - for the B4→B5 link to be valid.


(d) Blocks to recompute: [1 mark] Starting from Block #3 (tampered), the attacker must recompute: Block #3's hash, then Block #4's hash (which depends on Block #3's new PrevHash), then Block #5's hash. That is 3 blocks (Blocks #3, #4, and #5).

Marking note: For (b), award 1 mark for identifying the B4→B5 PrevHash mismatch ("ccc333" ≠ "ddd444") and 1 mark for reasoning back to conclude Block #3 was the original tampered block. Accept well-reasoned alternative interpretations if they correctly identify the chain break.
